fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J

The first applicant and second applicant apply for leave to commence adoption proceedings in respect of X born in 2013 pursuant to s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
X resides with her mother (the first applicant), her stepfather (the second applicant) and her two half-siblings, the children of the applicants.

Leave to apply for adoption

The process of adoption is governed by adoption legislation enacted by the legislatures of the state and territory governments in the Commonwealth of Australia. In the state of Queensland, the process of adoption is governed by the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld). However, a person may only apply to arrange an adoption of a stepchild if that person has been granted leave under the s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) to make an adoption application in respect of that child.
In Denforth & Sable [2023] FedCFamC1F 903, Carew J set out the less than straight forward statutory arrangements by which this court comes to have jurisdiction in such applications:
[3] Since 1 September 2021, the jurisdiction of this Court to determine an application for leave to commence adoption proceedings is dependent upon the proceedings being transferred from an inferior court, namely the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (formerly known as the Federal Circuit Court of Australia), a court of record, to this Court (formerly known as the Family Court of Australia), a superior court of record (see s 9(1)(a) and s 10(1)(a) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) (“FCFCOA Act”)). Since 1 September 2021, the proceedings can no longer be instituted in this Court, but because of a protocol between the Courts, such applications are generally transferred, such is the importance given to the jurisdiction relating to adoption.
[4] Section 60G of the FLA relevantly provides as follows:
(1) Subject to subsection (2), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), ... may grant leave for proceedings to be commenced for the adoption of a child by a prescribed adopting parent.

(2) In proceedings for leave under subsection (1), the court must consider whether granting leave would be in the child’s best interests, having regard to the effect of paragraph 60F(4)(a), or paragraph 60HA(3)(a), and of sections 61E and 65J.
[5] Section 25 of the FCFCOA Act relevantly provides as follows:
(1) The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) has original jurisdiction:
(a) if a matter, being the subject of a family law or child support proceeding, is transferred to the Court by the Court under section 51—as set out in paragraphs 132(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d); or

(b) if a matter, being the subject of a family law or child support proceeding, is transferred to the Court by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) under section 149—as set out in paragraphs 132(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d); or

(c) as is conferred on the Court, or in respect of which proceedings may be instituted in the Court, by any other Act.
[6] A “family law or child support proceeding” is defined in s 7 of the FCFCOA Act and means “proceedings in respect of which the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) has original jurisdiction under section 132.”

[7] The original jurisdiction of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) relevantly includes “matters in respect of which proceedings may be instituted under the Family Law Act 1975”.
The present application was commenced in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 19 November 2025 upon the first and second applicants filing an Initiating Application. It was transferred to this court and listed before me on 5 March 2026 by an order made by a Senior Judicial Registrar on 26 February 2026 pursuant to her delegated powers stipulated under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) sch 2, item 29.2A.
I am satisfied that I am seized of jurisdiction to determine this application.
The discretion reposed in the court by s 60G(1) of the Act is enlivened when the application is made by a prescribed adopting parent. A prescribed adopting parent, in relation to a child, means: a parent of the child; or the spouse of, or a person in a de facto relationship with, a parent of the child; or a parent of the child and either his or her spouse or a person in a de facto relationship with the parent. The first applicant is a parent of the child and the second applicant is a person in a de facto relationship with her (see the definition of prescribed adopting parent under section 4(1) of the Act). I am satisfied that the first and second applicants are prescribed adopting parents.
In proceedings for leave under s 60G(1), the court must consider whether granting leave would be in the child’s best interests, having regard to the effect of s 60F(4)(a), or s 60HA(3)(a), and of ss 61E and 65J of the Act (see s 60G(2) of the Act).
Section 60F(4)(a) provides that a child who would otherwise be considered a child of a marriage as prescribed by s 60F(1)(a) of the Act is no longer a child of that marriage where the child is adopted and leave under s 60G(1) of Act was granted in respect of that child. Section 60F(4)(a) has no application here, because the first applicant and the respondent never married.
Section 60H(3)(a) provides a child ceases to be a child of the person and the person’s de facto partner if a court granted leave under s 60G(1) for the adoption proceedings to be commenced in respect of that child (see also s 60HA(1)(a)). The first applicant deposes that she and the respondent commenced a relationship in or around January 2011, commenced cohabitation in July 2012 and separated on a final basis in March 2014. If I grant leave to adopt and she is adopted by the second applicant, she will cease to be a child of the first applicant and the respondent as former de facto partners. I take that into account.
Section 61E deals with the effect of adoption upon a person’s parental responsibility for the child adopted. If there has been a grant of leave under s 60G(2) of the Act then the effect of an adoption will be to remove from a person who had parental responsibility for the child adopted, that parental responsibility. A literal reading of s 61E(2) of the Act would mean that in this case, the first applicant’s and the respondent’s parental responsibility for X will end upon her adoption by the second applicant (see Cahill & Fryar [2008] FamCA 1245 at [13], Brock & Brock [2007] FamCA 1594 at [12], Tanner & Cape [2011] FamCA 665 at [15] and the other cases referred to therein). Despite the text of s 61E(2), in Hanlon & Hanlon [2022] FedCFamC1F 435, Gill J concluded that on its proper construction, s 61E of the Act did not operate to strip parental responsibility from the parent who was partnered with the adopting step-parent (see also Haskova & Viraj [2025] FedCFamC1F 103, at [19]).
I accept that the view expressed in Hanlon and Haskova is the preferable view of the work done by s 61E(2) of the Act and in the event that X is adopted by the second applicant, only the respondent’s parental responsibility for her will end, not that of the first applicant. I take that into account.
There is no parenting order made under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) in place for X. Section 65J of the Act does not arise for consideration.

The parties

The first applicant was born in 1993 and is currently 32 years of age. She is the biological mother of the child and works as a health professional.
The second applicant was born in 1993 and is currently 32 years of age. He is in a de facto relationship with the first applicant and works in manufacturing.
The respondent to the proceedings, Mr Eglite, is X’s biological father. The first applicant deposes that in or around October 2018 X requested to have her surname changed from ‘Eglite’, the respondent’s surname to ‘Bocker’, the second applicant’s surname. The respondent consented to this change in January 2025.
The first applicant deposes that the respondent has not exercised regular contact with X, nor has he had meaningful involvement in her life since 2 October 2016.
Mr Eglite was served with the Initiating Application filed by the applicants on 19 November 2025 and the documents in support of the application on 4 December 2025. The respondent signed an Acknowledgement of Service to this effect but has not otherwise participated in these proceedings.
Further, the solicitors for the applicant’s gave the respondent notice of the hearing today, 5 March 2026.

X

X is the biological child of the first applicant and the respondent. The first applicant and the respondent commenced a relationship in 2011, later commencing cohabitation in 2012 before separating on a final basis in March 2014.
The first applicant deposes that the respondent conveyed to her that he wished to re-establish a relationship with X and that he would be prepared to consent to the second applicant adopting her by letter correspondence dated 10 September 2025. No evidence of the correspondence is provided, but the second applicant asserts that X has made it clear she does not want a relationship with the respondent at this time and has assured her that he and the first applicant would fully support her if she wished to develop a relationship with the respondent in the future.
From the text message correspondence annexed to the first applicant’s affidavit filed 19 November 2025, the respondent acquiesces to and offers support for the adoption of X (annexure MSA-03 of the affidavit).
The applicants commenced a relationship in 2014. X and the applicants have “resided together as a family” since then and with X’s half-brother B since his birth in 2018 and her half-sister C since her birth in 2022.
The first and second applicants have consistently provided and provide for X’s emotional, financial and physical support and the second applicant deposes that he and X have developed “an extremely close and loving bond” and have shared experiences which “have strengthened [their] connection as father and daughter”. I am satisfied that the first and second applicants have the capacity to provide for and attend to the child’s developmental, psychological and emotional needs.
X is presently 12 years of age. Both applicants depose to X’s wish to be adopted by the second applicant, a wish that the first applicant says “she has consistently expressed”. The applicants depose that X views the second applicant as her father. An adoption will change her legal status to reflect the reality of her close relationship with him.

Disposition

Having regard to the above matters, I consider that leave to commence adoption proceedings should be granted and will so order.
